steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The student claims that traffic congestion rose over time in both very large cities and smaller areas, and that smaller areas were less affected than very large cities. A supporting choice can use one smaller-area example if it clearly shows both the rising trend and the lower level compared with Philadelphia.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — Even though the amount of traffic delay per commuter per year was greater in the Albany-Schenectady-Troy, New York, area than in the Philadelphia, Pennsylvania, area throughout the period from 1990 to 2000, the amounts were nearly identical in 1996.: Reverses the comparison — it says Albany was greater than Philadelphia, which contradicts the claim that smaller areas were less affected. ✗
  • Choice B — In 1992, the amount of traffic delay in the Philadelphia, Pennsylvania, area was less than 30 hours per commuter per year.: Only describes one data point in Philadelphia. It says nothing about smaller areas, their relative level, or whether congestion rose. ✗
  • Choice C — In at least one of the three urban areas shown, the amount of traffic delay was less than 20 hours per commuter per year at one point between 1990 and 2000.: Notes a single low-value moment but does not address the rising trend or the persistent gap between large and small areas. ✗
  • Choice D — While the annual number of hours of traffic delay per commuter was always lower in the Albany-Schenectady-Troy, New York, area than in the Philadelphia, Pennsylvania, area for each year between 1990 and 2000, the amount of traffic delay rose in both areas during this period.: Shows that Albany, a smaller area, stayed below Philadelphia and still rose over time, directly supporting the claim. ✓
The Albany comparison supplies a smaller-area example that supports the student's claim.
